question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2025

Code: 55/7/3 (set-3)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 31 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 33 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

PHYSICS (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 33 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five sections - Sections {tex}\boldsymbol{A}, \boldsymbol{B}, \boldsymbol{C}, \boldsymbol{D}{/tex} and {tex}\boldsymbol{E}{/tex}.
  3. In Section A: Question numbers 1 to 16 are Multiple Choice type questions. Each question carries 1 mark.
  4. In Section B: Question numbers 17 to 21 are Very Short Answer type questions. Each question carries 2 marks.
  5. In Section C: Question numbers 22 to 28 are Short Answer type questions. Each question carries 3 marks.
  6. In Section D: Question numbers 29 & 30 are case study-based questions. Each question carries 4 marks.
  7. In Section E: Question numbers 31 to 33 are Long Answer type questions. Each question carries 5 marks.
  8. There is no overall choice given in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in few questions in all the Sections except Section A.
  9. Kindly note that there is a separate question paper for Visually Impaired candidates.
  10. Use of calculators is not allowed.

    {tex} c=3 \times 10^8 {~m} / {s} {/tex}
    {tex} {h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} {Js} {/tex}
    {tex} {e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C} {/tex}

Section - A
1.

In the circuit shown, the charge on the left plate of {tex}20 \mu {~F}{/tex} capacitor is {tex}-50 \mu {C}{/tex}. The charge on the right plate of the {tex}12 \mu {~F}{/tex} capacitor is:

a. {tex}20 \mu {C}{/tex}
b. {tex}-20 \mu {C}{/tex}
c. {tex}30 \mu {C}{/tex}
d. {tex}-30 \mu {C}{/tex}
1
2.

Germanium crystal is doped at room temperature with a minute quantity of boron. The charge carriers in the doped semiconductors will be:

a. electrons only
b. holes only
c. holes and few electrons
d. electrons and few holes
1
3.

A steady current I is passed through a conductor at room temperature for time t. It is observed that its temperature rises by {tex}0 \cdot 5^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. If 2 I current is passed through the conductor (at room temperature) for the same duration, the rise in its temperature will be approximately:

a. {tex}1 \cdot 0^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
b. {tex}1.5^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
c. {tex}2 \cdot 0^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
d. {tex}4 \cdot 0^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
1
4.

The dimensions of 'self-inductance' are:

a. {tex}\left[{M} {L} {T}^{-2} {~A}^{-2}\right]{/tex}
b. {tex}\left[{M} {L}^{2} {~T}^{-1} {~A}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
c. {tex}\left[{M} {L}^{-1} {~T}^{-2} {~A}^{-2}\right]{/tex}
d. {tex}\left[{M} {L}^{2} {~T}^{-2} {~A}^{-2}\right]{/tex}
1
5.

Isotones are the nuclides having:

a. same mass numbers
b. same atomic numbers
c. same neutron number, but different atomic number
d. different neutron number, and different mass number
1
6.

A metal rod of length 50 cm is held vertically and moved with a velocity of {tex}10 {~m} / {s}{/tex} towards east. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at the place is {tex}0 \cdot 4 {G}{/tex}. The emf induced across the ends of the rod is:

a. 0.1 mV
b. 0.2 mV
c. 0.8 mV
d. 1.6 mV
1
7.

The frequency of a photon of energy {tex}1 \cdot 326 {eV}{/tex} is:

a. {tex}1.18 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex}
b. {tex}3.20 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex}
c. {tex}4.20 \times 10^{15} {~Hz}{/tex}
d. {tex}4.80 \times 10^{15} {~Hz}{/tex}
1
8.

An alternating current is given by {tex}{I}={I}_{0} \cos (100 \pi) {t}{/tex}. The least time the current takes to decrease from its maximum value to zero will be:

a. {tex}\left(\frac{1}{200}\right) {s}{/tex}
b. {tex}\left(\frac{1}{150}\right) {s}{/tex}
c. {tex}\left(\frac{1}{100}\right) {s}{/tex}
d. {tex}\left(\frac{1}{50}\right) {s}{/tex}
1
9.

A rectangular coil of area A is kept in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} such that the plane of the coil makes an angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}. The magnetic flux linked with the coil is:

a. {tex}{BA} \sin \alpha{/tex}
b. {tex}{BA} \cos \alpha{/tex}
c. BA
d. zero
1
10.

The minimum energy required to free the electron from the ground state of the hydrogen atom is:

a. {tex}3 \cdot 4 {eV}{/tex}
b. less than 3.4 eV
c. {tex}13 \cdot 6 {eV}{/tex}
d. between 13.6 eV and 3.4 eV
1
11.

A capacitor and an inductor are connected in series across an ac source of voltage of variable frequency. The frequency is increased continuously. The nature of the circuit before and after the resonance will be:

a. inductive only
b. capacitive only
c. capacitive and inductive respectively
d. inductive and capacitive respectively
1
12.

A p-n junction diode is forward biased. As a result,

a. both the potential barrier height and the width of depletion layer decrease.
b. both the potential barrier height and the width of depletion layer increase.
c. the potential barrier height decreases and the width of depletion layer increases.
d. the potential barrier height increases and the width of depletion layer decreases.
1

Questions number 13 to 16 are Assertion (A) and Reason (R) type questions. Two statements are given - one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are false.
13.

Assertion (A): EM waves do not require a medium for their propagation.
Reason (R): EM waves are transverse waves.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
14.

Assertion (A): A charged particle is moving with velocity v in {tex}{x}-{y}{/tex} plane, making an angle {tex}\theta\left(0 \lt \theta \lt \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} with x-axis. If a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} is applied in the region, along {tex}y{/tex}-axis, the particle will move in a helical path with its axis parallel to x-axis.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: The direction of the magnetic force acting on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field is along the velocity of the particle.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. Both A and R are false.
1
15.

Assertion (A): The minimum negative potential applied to the anode in a photoelectric experiment at which photoelectric current becomes zero, is called cut-off voltage.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: The threshold frequency for a metal is the minimum frequency of incident radiation below which emission of photoelectrons does not take place.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
16.

Assertion (A): A ray of light is incident normally on the face of a prism. The emergent ray will graze along the opposite face of the prism when the critical angle at glass-air interface is equal to the angle of the prism.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: The refractive index of a prism depends on angle of the prism.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1

17.

Calculate the value of the current passing through the battery in the given circuit diagram.

 2
18.

In a Young's double-slit experiment, the intensity at the central maximum in the interference pattern on the screen is {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex}. Find the intensity at a point on the screen where the path difference between the interfering waves is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{6}{/tex}.

 2
19.

Define the 'distance of closest approach'. An {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle of kinetic energy K is bombarded on a thin gold foil. Derive an expression for the 'distance of closest approach'.

 2
20.(i)

Using the mirror equation and the formula of magnification, deduce that "the virtual image produced by a convex mirror is always diminished in size and is located between the pole and the focus."

 2

OR
(ii)

A convex lens of focal length 10 cm, a concave lens of focal length 15 cm and a third lens of unknown focal length are placed coaxially in contact. If the focal length of the combination is +12 cm, find the nature and focal length of the third lens, if all lenses are thin. Will the answer change if the lenses were thick?

 2
21.

Draw energy band diagrams of n-type and p-type semiconductors at temperature T > 0 K. Show the donor/acceptor energy levels with the order of difference of their energies from the bands.

 2

Section - C
22.

Explain the process of formation of 'depletion layer' and 'potential barrier' in a p-n junction region of a diode, with the help of a suitable diagram. Which feature of junction diode makes it suitable for its use as a rectifier?

 3
23.

An electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} given by:
{tex}\vec{E} =100 \hat{i} \frac{N}{C} \text { for } x \gt 0{/tex}
{tex}=-100 \hat{i} \frac{N}{C} \text { for } x \lt 0{/tex}
exists in a region. A right circular cylinder of length 10 cm and radius 2 cm, is placed in the region such that its axis coincides with x -axis and its two faces are at {tex}x=-5 {~cm}{/tex} and {tex}x=5 {~cm}{/tex}. Calculate:

  1. the net outward flux through the cylinder, and
  2. the net charge inside the cylinder.

24.

Answer the following, giving reasons:

  1. The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of incident radiation.
  2. Photoelectric current increases with the increase in the intensity of the incident radiation.
  3. The stopping potential {tex}{V}_{0}{/tex} varies linearly with the frequency {tex}v{/tex} of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface.

25.(i)
  1. Write Biot-Savart's law in vector form.
  2. Two identical circular coils A and B, each of radius R , carrying currents {tex}I{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{3} I{/tex} respectively, are placed concentrically in XY and YZ planes respectively. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at their common centre.
 3

OR
(ii)
  1. A rectangular loop of sides {tex}l{/tex} and b carries a current I clockwise. Write the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} of the loop and show its direction in a diagram.
  2. The loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} and is free to rotate about an axis which is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}. Prove that the loop experiences no net force, but a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}.
 3
26.

Name the electromagnetic radiations and write their frequency range:

  1. Used to take the photograph of the bones
  2. Produced by hot bodies
  3. Used in television communication systems
 3
27.

An ac source of voltage is connected to a circuit element X. It is observed that the current flowing through X varies as {tex}I=I_0 \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) .{/tex}

  1. Identify the element X and write the expression for its reactance.
  2. Plot a graph to show the variation of reactance of the element with the frequency of the applied voltage.
  3. Draw plots showing the variation of voltage and current with time over one cycle of applied ac.
 3
28.

Write the mathematical forms of three postulates of Bohr's theory of the hydrogen atom. Using them prove that, for an electron revolving in the {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit,

  1. the radius of the orbit is proportional to {tex}{n}^{2}{/tex}, and
  2. the total energy of the atom is proportional to {tex}\left(\frac{1}{{n}^{2}}\right){/tex}.
 3

Section - D

Questions number 29 and 30 are case study-based questions. Read the following paragraphs and answer the questions that follow.
29.

Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
In a metallic conductor, an electron, moving due to thermal motion, suffers collisions with the heavy fixed ions but after collision, it will emerge out with the same speed but in random directions. If we consider all the electrons, their average velocity will be zero. When an electric field is applied, electrons move with an average velocity, known as drift velocity ({tex}{v}_{{d}}{/tex}). The average time between successive collisions is known as relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex}. The magnitude of drift velocity per unit electric field is called mobility ({tex}\mu{/tex}).
An expression for current through the conductor can be obtained in terms of drift velocity, number of electrons per unit volume (n), electronic charge (-e), and the cross-sectional area of the conductor. This expression leads to an expression between current density {tex}(\hat{j}){/tex} and the electric field ({tex}\vec{E}{/tex}). Hence, an expression for resistivity ({tex}\rho{/tex}) of a metal is obtained. This expression helps us to understand increase in resistivity of a metal with increase in its temperature, in terms of change in the relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex} and change in the number density of electrons {tex}({n}){/tex}.

  1. Consider two cylindrical conductors A and B, made of the same metal connected in series to a battery. The length and the radius of B are twice that of A. If {tex}\mu_{{A}}{/tex} and {tex}\mu_{{B}}{/tex} are the mobility of electrons in A and {tex}B{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\frac{\mu_A}{\mu_B}{/tex} is:
    1. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
    3. 2
    4. 1
  2. A wire of length 0.5 m and cross-sectional area {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^2{/tex} is connected to a battery of 2 V that maintains a current of 1.5 A in it. The conductivity of the material of the wire (in {tex}\Omega^{-1} {~m}^{-1}{/tex}) is:
    1. {tex}2.5 \times 10^4{/tex}
    2. {tex}3.0 \times 10^5{/tex}
    3. {tex}3.75 \times 10^6{/tex}
    4. {tex}5.0 \times 10^7{/tex}
  3. The temperature coefficient of resistance of nichrome is {tex}1.70 \times 10^{-4}{ }^{\circ} {C}^{-1}{/tex}. In order to increase resistance of a nichrome wire by {tex}8.5 \%{/tex}, the temperature of the wire should be increased by:
    1. {tex}250^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    2. {tex}500^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    3. {tex}850^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    4. {tex}1000^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    1. Consider the contribution of the following two factors I and II in resistivity of a metal:
      1. Relaxation time of electrons
      2. Number of electrons per unit volume
        The resistivity of a metal increases with increase in its temperature because:
        1. I decreases and II increases.
        2. I increases and II is almost constant.
        3. Both I and II increase.
        4. I decreases and II is almost constant.

          OR

    2. A steady current flows in a copper wire of non-uniform cross-section. Consider the following three physical quantities:
      1. Electric field
      2. Current density
      3. Drift speed
        Then at the different points along the wire:
        1. II and III change, but I is constant.
        2. I and II change, but III is constant.
        3. I and III change, but II is constant.
        4. All I, II and III change.
 4
30.

Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
When light travels from an optically denser medium to an optically rarer medium, at the interface it is partly reflected back into the same medium and partly refracted to the second medium. The angle of incidence corresponding to an angle of refraction {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} is called the critical angle ({tex}{i}_{{c}}{/tex}) for the given pair of media. This angle is related to the refractive index of medium 1 with respect to medium 2.
Refraction of light through a prism involves refraction at two plane interfaces. A relation for the refractive index of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of the refracting angle of the prism and the angle of minimum deviation. For a thin prism, this relation reduces to a simple equation.
Laws of refraction are also valid for refraction of light at a spherical interface. When an object is placed in front of a spherical surface separating two media, its image is formed. A relation between object and image distance, in terms of refractive indices of two media and the radius of curvature of the spherical surface can be obtained. Using this relation for two surfaces of a lens, 'lens maker formula' is obtained.

  1. A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing a transparent liquid (refractive index n) to a depth H. The radius of the circular area of the surface of liquid, through which light from the bulb can emerge out, is R. Then {tex}\left(\frac{{R}}{{H}}\right){/tex} is: (1)
    1. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{n}^2-1}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\sqrt{{n}^2-1}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{n}^2+1}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\sqrt{{n}^2+1}{/tex}
    1. A parallel beam of light is incident on a face of a prism with refracting angle {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}. The angle of minimum deviation is found to be {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}. The refractive index of the material of the prism is close to: (1)
      1. {tex}1.3{/tex}
      2. {tex}1.4{/tex}
      3. 1.5
      4. 1.6

        OR

    2. The angle of minimum deviation for a ray of light incident on a thin prism, made of crown glass {tex}(n=1.52){/tex} is {tex}D_m{/tex}. If the prism was made of dense flint glass ({tex}{n}=1.62{/tex}) instead of crown glass, the angle of minimum deviation will: (1)
      1. decrease by {tex}4 \%{/tex}
      2. increase by {tex}4 \%{/tex}
      3. decrease by 19%
      4. increase by 19%
  3. An object is placed in front of a convex spherical glass surface ({tex}{n}=1.5{/tex} and radius of curvature R) at a distance of 4 R from it. As the object is moved slowly close to the surface, the image formed is: (1)
    1. always real
    2. always virtual
    3. first real and then virtual
    4. first virtual and then real
  4. A double-convex lens, made of glass of refractive index {tex}1.5{/tex}, has focal length 10 cm. The radius of curvature of its each face, is: (1)
    1. 10 cm
    2. 15 cm
    3. 20 cm
    4. 40 cm
 4

Section - E
31.(i)
  1. With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the principle of working of a moving coil galvanometer. Write the purpose of using (I) radial magnetic field, and (II) soft iron core, in it.
  2. Define current sensitivity of a galvanometer. "Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity." Give reason.
 5

OR
(ii)
    1. Write Ampere's circuital law in mathematical form and explain the terms used.
    2. As the current carrying solenoid is made longer, the magnetic field produced outside it approaches zero. Why?
    3. A flexible loop of irregular shape carrying current when located in an external magnetic field, changes to a circular shape. Give reason.
  2. A galvanometer of resistance G is converted into a voltmeter to measure up to V volts, by connecting a resistance {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex} in series with the coil. If {tex}R_{1}{/tex} is replaced by {tex}R_{2}{/tex}, then it can only measure up to {tex}\frac{{V}}{2}{/tex} volt. Find the value of the resistance {tex}{R}_{3}{/tex} (in terms of {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}{/tex}) needed to convert it into a voltmeter that can read up to 2 V.
 5
32.(i)
  1. Explain with the help of a labelled ray diagram the formation of final image by an astronomical telescope at infinity. Write the expression for its magnifying power.
  2. The total magnification produced by a compound microscope is 20. The magnification produced by the eyepiece is 5. When the microscope is focussed on a certain object, the distance between the objective and eyepiece is observed to be 14 cm. Calculate the focal lengths of the objective and the eyepiece. (Given that the least distance of distinct vision {tex}=25 {~cm}{/tex})
 5

OR
(ii)
  1. Two coherent light waves, each of intensity {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex} superpose each other and produce interference pattern on a screen. Obtain the expression for the resultant intensity at a point where the phase difference between the waves is {tex}\phi{/tex}. Write its maximum and minimum possible values.
  2. In a single slit diffraction experiment, the aperture of the slit is 3 mm and the separation between the slit and the screen is 1.5 m. A monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm is normally incident on the slit. Calculate the distance of
    1. first order minimum, and
    2. second order maximum, from the centre of the screen.
 5
33.(i)
  1. A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation {tex}d{/tex} has a capacitance {tex}C_{0}{/tex}. A slab of dielectric constant {tex}K{/tex} having area A and thickness {tex}\left(\frac{d}{4}\right){/tex} is inserted in the capacitor, parallel to the plates. Find the new value of its capacitance.
  2. You are provided with a large number of {tex}1 \mu {~F}{/tex} identical capacitors and a power supply of 1200 V. The dielectric medium used in each capacitor can withstand up to 200 V only. Find the minimum number of capacitors and their arrangement, required to build a capacitor system of equivalent capacitance of {tex}2 \mu {~F}{/tex} for use with this supply.
 5

OR
(ii)
  1. An electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}p{/tex} consists of point charges q and -q, separated by 2 a. Derive an expression for electric potential in terms of its dipole moment at a point at a distance {tex}{x}(\gg {a}){/tex} from its centre and lying
    1. along its axis, and
    2. along its bisector line.
  2. An electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=(0.8 \hat{i}+0.6 \hat{j}) 10^{-29} {Cm}{/tex} is placed in an electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=1.0 \times 10^{7} \hat{{k}} \frac{{V}}{{m}}{/tex}. Calculate the magnitude of the torque acting on it and the angle it makes with the x-axis, at this instant.
 5

Section - C
22.

Explain the process of formation of 'depletion layer' and 'potential barrier' in a p-n junction region of a diode, with the help of a suitable diagram. Which feature of junction diode makes it suitable for its use as a rectifier?

Solution:

When an electron diffuses from n-side to p -side, it leaves behind an ionized donor on n side.
Similarly when a hole diffuses from p-side to n-side, it leaves behind an ionized acceptor on {tex}p{/tex} side.
This space charge region consisting of immobile ions on either side of the junction is known as depletion layer.
As diffusion process continues, width of depletion layer increases and consequently strength of electric field increases across the junction and thus the drift current.
The potential that prevents the movement of electron from {tex}n{/tex} region into p region is called potential barrier.

Diode allows current to pass only when it is forward biased as resistance is small, whereas in reverse bias, its resistance is very large.

  2. In a single slit diffraction experiment, the aperture of the slit is 3 mm and the separation between the slit and the screen is 1.5 m. A monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm is normally incident on the slit. Calculate the distance of
    1. first order minimum, and

Solution:
  1. {tex} y_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex}
    {tex} y_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex}
    According to Principle of Superposition
    {tex} {y} =y_1+y_2 {/tex}
    {tex} =a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)] {/tex}
    {tex} =2 a \cos \phi / 2 \cos (\omega t+\phi / 2) {/tex}
    {tex} {y}=\operatorname{Acos}(\omega {t}+\phi / 2) {/tex}
    where, {tex}{A}=2 a \cos \phi / 2{/tex}
    {tex} I=k A^2 {/tex}
    {tex} I=k\left(4 a^2 \cos ^2 \phi / 2\right) {/tex}
    {tex} I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex}
    Maximum value {tex}I=4 I_0{/tex}
    Minimum value {tex}I=0{/tex}
    1. Position of first order minimum
      {tex} y =\frac{n \lambda D}{a} {/tex}
      {tex} y_1 =\frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{3 \times 10^{-3}}=3 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex}
    2. Position of second order maximum
      {tex} y_n=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda D}{2 a} {/tex}
      {tex} n=2, y_2 =\frac{5 \lambda D}{2 a} {/tex}

Solution:
  1. {tex} {C}_0 =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}} {/tex}
    {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{({~d}-{t})+\frac{{t}}{K}} {/tex}
    {tex} {t} ={d} / 4 {/tex}
    {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{\left({~d}-\frac{{d}}{4}\right)+\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}\left(\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4 {~K}}\right)} {/tex}
    {tex} ={C}_0 \frac{4 {~K}}{(3 {~K}+1)} {/tex}
  2. Each capacitance can withstand 200 V
    No. of capacitors in each row {tex}=\frac{1200}{200}=6{/tex}
    Net capacitance of each row {tex}=1 / 6 \mu {~F}{/tex}
    Number of rows {tex}={n}{/tex}
    {tex} C_{e q} =C_1+C_2+------+C_n {/tex}
    {tex} C_{e q} =\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+-----n {/tex}
    {tex} 2 =\frac{n}{6} {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore n=12 {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Total no. of capacitors in the arrangement } =6 \times 12 {/tex}
    {tex} =72 {/tex}

(i) Using the mirror equation and the formula of magnification, deduce that "the virtual image produced by a convex mirror is always diminished in size and is located between the pole and the focus." Solution: Let, {tex}{u}={nf}{/tex} From the Mirror formula, {tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{n f} {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{n f}{n+1} {/tex} {tex} n =+v e \therefore v<f {/tex} {tex} m =\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{1}{n+1} {/tex} m is always positive & less than 1. 2 OR (ii) A convex lens of focal length 10 cm, a concave lens of focal length 15 cm and a third lens of unknown focal length are placed coaxially in contact. Solution: {tex} \frac{1}{{f}} =\frac{1}{{f}_1}+\frac{1}{{f}_2}+\frac{1}{{f}_3} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{12} =\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{{f}_3} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{f}_3} =\frac{5-6+4}{60} {/tex} {tex} {f}_3 =20 {~cm} {/tex} Nature: Convex Yes 2 21. Draw energy band diagrams of n-type and p-type semiconductors at temperature T > 0 K. Show the donor/acceptor energy levels with the order of difference of their energies from the bands. Solution: 2 Section - C 22. Explain the process of formation of 'depletion layer' and 'potential barrier' in a p-n junction region of a diode, with the help of a suitable diagram. Which feature of junction diode makes it suitable for its use as a rectifier? Solution: When an electron diffuses from n-side to p -side, it leaves behind an ionized donor on n side. Similarly when a hole diffuses from p-side to n-side, it leaves behind an ionized acceptor on {tex}p{/tex} side. This space charge region consisting of immobile ions on either side of the junction is known as depletion layer. As diffusion process continues, width of depletion layer increases and consequently strength of electric field increases across the junction and thus the drift current. The potential that prevents the movement of electron from {tex}n{/tex} region into p region is called potential barrier. Diode allows current to pass only when it is forward biased as resistance is small, whereas in reverse bias, its resistance is very large. 3 23. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} given by: {tex}\vec{E} =100 \hat{i} \frac{N}{C} \text { for } x \gt 0{/tex} {tex}=-100 \hat{i} \frac{N}{C} \text { for } x \lt 0{/tex} exists in a region. Solution: {tex} \phi_L =\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta S} {/tex} {tex} =100(-\hat{i}) \cdot \Delta S(-\hat{i}) {/tex} {tex} =100 \times \pi\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =4 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \phi_R =100(\hat{i}) \cdot \Delta S(\hat{i}) {/tex} {tex} =100 \times \pi\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =4 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \phi_{\text {total }} =\phi_{{L}}+\phi_{{R}} {/tex} {tex} =8 \pi \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} =25.12 \times 10^{-2} {Nm}^2 / {C} {/tex} {tex} \text { Charge } {/tex} {tex} q =\varepsilon_0 \phi_{\text {total }} {/tex} {tex} =25.12 \times 10^{-2} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} {/tex} {tex} =0.22 \times 10^{-11} {C} {/tex} 3 24. Answer the following, giving reasons: The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of incident radiation. Photoelectric current increases with the increase in the intensity of the incident radiation. The stopping potential {tex}{V}_{0}{/tex} varies linearly with the frequency {tex}v{/tex} of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface. Solution: With the variation in the intensity of incident radiations, there is a change in the number of photons not on the energies of the photons so the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons remains same. With the increase in intensity of incident radiation number of photons increases as a result photoelectric current increase. With the increase in frequency of incident radiation , the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons increases. Hence higher value of stopping potential (retarding potential) is required. 3 25. (i) Write Biot-Savart's law in vector form. Two identical circular coils A and B, each of radius R , carrying currents {tex}I{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{3} I{/tex} respectively, are placed concentrically in XY and YZ planes respectively. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at their common centre. Solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{{dB}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^3}{/tex} {tex} {B}_1 =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B}_2 =\frac{\mu_0 \sqrt{3} {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B} =\sqrt{{B}_1^2+{B}_2^2} {/tex} {tex} \therefore {~B} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} \sqrt{1+3} {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 I}{R} {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{B_1}{B_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex} Direction of net magnetic field is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} with direction of {tex}{B}_2 / 60^{\circ}{/tex} with the direction of {tex}B_1{/tex} . 3 OR (ii) A rectangular loop of sides {tex}l{/tex} and b carries a current I clockwise. Prove that the loop experiences no net force, but a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} . Solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{{m}}={I} \overrightarrow{{A}}{/tex} {tex} {F}_1={F}_2={IbB} {/tex} {tex}{F}_1=\text { Force on } {AB} \text { into the plane } {/tex} {tex} {F}_2=\text { Force on } {CD} \text { out of the plane } {/tex} Since forces are equal & opposite so net force {tex}=0{/tex} Both forces form a couple, magnitude of torque acting on the coil is {tex} \therefore \tau ={F}_1 \frac{l}{2} \sin \theta+{F}_2 \frac{l}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={I} {b} {B} l \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={I} {A} {B} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={m} {B} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex} 3 26. Name the electromagnetic radiations and write their frequency range: Used to take the photograph of the bones Produced by hot bodies Used in television communication systems Solution: Name: X-rays Frequency Range: {tex}10^{16}-10^{20} {~Hz}{/tex} Name: Infra-red radiation Frequency Range: {tex}10^{12}-10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} Name: Radio waves Frequency Range: {tex}10^5-10^9 {~Hz}{/tex} 3 27. An ac source of voltage is connected to a circuit element X.
